food_subgroup: Steamed or fried stuffed dumplings with vegetable filling

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic dumpling food product class (FOODON_00002473). No specific momo or South Asian dumpling class exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers dumplings generically. No specific momo subcategory exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for momo or South Asian vegetable dumplings identified.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of veg momos is 6 pieces, weighing approximately 180 g, as commonly served at Indian and Nepali restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Steamed Vegetable Dumpling / Momo (wheat flour dough, vegetable filling)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for steamed wheat-flour dumplings with vegetable filling used as primary basis. Fried variant macros adjusted upward for fat and energy based on ICMR-NIN frying absorption data (approximately +4–6 g fat per 100 g for shallow-fried dumplings). Values presented represent a weighted average of steamed and fried preparation, consistent with the dual-preparation menu item. Sodium estimated from typical recipe salt addition per ICMR-NIN recipe standards. Secondary cross-check performed against USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Dumplings, vegetable, steamed' (FDC ID 2103689).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — steamed wheat-flour dumplings / dim sum analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for veg momos found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 estimated by analogy with steamed wheat-flour dumplings (dim sum / jiaozi type) reported in Atkinson FS et al. (2021) 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021', American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, which lists steamed wheat-flour dumplings at GI ~45–52 (mean ~48). The vegetable filling and moderate fat content further moderate glycemic response. Fried preparation raises fat content slightly but does not substantially alter GI. GL per serving calculated as (48 × 54.9 g carbohydrate in 180 g serving) / 100 = 26.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~558 mg per 6-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 6-piece serving of veg momos contributes approximately 558 mg sodium (~24% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or chilli sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low GI (48); refined wheat flour dough
type2_diabetes.guidance: Veg momos have a low GI of approximately 48 due to the dense wheat-flour wrapper and vegetable filling, but the glycemic load per 6-piece serving is approximately 26 g, which is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to 3–4 pieces per meal and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.9 g per 100 g); no cholesterol; vegetable filling
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed veg momos are low in saturated fat and contain no cholesterol, making them a relatively heart-friendly choice; however, the fried variant adds approximately 4–6 g additional fat per 100 g, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer the steamed preparation and avoid deep-fried versions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Veg momos are steamed or fried dumplings originating from Tibetan and Nepali cuisine, widely popularised across North India and now served at Indian restaurants throughout Singapore. The dumpling wrapper is made from refined wheat flour (maida) kneaded into a thin, pliable dough, which is filled with a spiced mixture of finely chopped cabbage, carrot, onion, ginger, garlic, green chilli, and fresh coriander. In the steamed version, the filled parcels are cooked in a bamboo or metal steamer for 10–12 minutes, yielding a soft, slightly chewy wrapper with a moist, aromatic vegetable interior. In the fried version, the dumplings are pan-fried or deep-fried after steaming or directly from raw, producing a golden, crispy exterior. A standard serving is 6 pieces (approximately 180 g). Nutritionally, veg momos provide approximately 195 kcal per 100 g, with 5.8 g protein, 5.2 g fat, and 30.5 g carbohydrate. They are a source of dietary fibre (2.2 g per 100 g) from the vegetable filling. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low), making them a moderate glycemic-load food at a 6-piece serving. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 310 mg per 100 g, primarily from added salt and any dipping sauce. Veg momos are vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, but contain gluten. They are typically served with a spicy tomato-chilli chutney or sesame-soy dipping sauce. Clinically, the steamed variant is preferable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease or weight, while both variants require portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension.
common_user_queries: How many calories in veg momos?
What is the GI of momos?
Are momos good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for veg momos as a named dish exists; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN wheat-flour steamed dumpling / vegetable filling data with frying-absorption adjustment. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using steamed wheat-flour dumplings as the reference food. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient profile and preparation method are well-characterised and the analogous sources are closely matched.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a weighted average of steamed and fried preparation as the menu item is listed as 'Steamed/Fried'. Steamed variant will have approximately 4–6 g less fat per 100 g and approximately 30–40 fewer kcal per 100 g than the fried variant. 2. Serving size of 180 g (6 pieces at ~30 g each) is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion weights for momos in Singapore; actual weight may vary by restaurant. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based food with no UV-exposed mushrooms or fortification; confirmed zero by formulation. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based food; confirmed zero by formulation. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation; confirmed zero by formulation. 8. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for veg momos is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 9. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input record appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded; veg momos are not related to butter chicken. 10. GL per serving = (48 × (30.5 × 1.8)) / 100 = (48 × 54.9) / 100 = 26.35, rounded to 26.3.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Assigned ICMR-NIN as primary nutrient source with USDA FDC cross-check. Estimated GI by analogy with steamed wheat-flour dumplings from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021. Populated all mandatory multilingual fields for Singaporean-cuisine category (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Documented all -1 sentinel values (selenium_mcg) and all confirmed-zero values (vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg, cholesterol_mg, trans_fat_g) in notes. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Disregarded erroneous 'Butter Chicken' cuisine tag from input. Self-scored 8/8 with all hard gates passing. Decision: PASS.
